clean_story:
Sunlight in pockets. Time standardized by salt.

The word hour comes from the Greek hōra, meaning "season" or "time," but its spread had less to do with clocks and more with cargo. Before trade, time was local—noon struck when the sun hung highest over your village. A merchant’s camel train, though, couldn’t run on "whenever the shadow looks right." Salt caravans crossing the Sahara used water clocks (clay bowls with holes) to measure stops, but these dripped faster in desert heat. By 1300, Italian merchants—trading wool for spices—demanded consistency. Their solution? Divide daylight into 12 equal hours, winter or summer, using mechanical clocks in port cities like Venice. Ships carried these clocks alongside silk and pepper, syncing docks from Bruges to Beijing. The real enforcer? Railways in 1840. Trains crashing into each other over "your noon" vs. "my noon" forced Britain to adopt Railway Time—one clock for all. Trade didn’t just move goods; it moved the hands of the clock.

Three related subjects are spice route economics, mechanical clock precision, and how latitude bends time.
